title: The man who sells ferry tickets on the island
summary: Describe a worker who has done the same small job for decades and seems to hold a whole island together.
topic: A worker who has done the same modest job for many years
prompt: Write 9–11 sentences describing someone who has done the same modest job for a very long time (a ticket seller, a ferry man, a market weigher). Describe his routine, his manner, and why people rely on him. Use present simple, present perfect for duration, and adjectives of character.
hintStarters: Kostas has sold ferry tickets from the same small window for…
Every morning he opens the shutter at exactly…
He knows everyone by name, and he always remembers…
He could have retired years ago, but he says…
sampleText: Kostas has sold ferry tickets from the same small window for almost forty years. The window is by the harbour on a Greek island, and the paint around it has turned soft blue from the salt and the sun. Every morning he opens the shutter at exactly half past six, before the first boat arrives. He is a calm and patient man, and he never seems to hurry, even when the queue is long. He knows almost everyone on the island by name, and he often remembers which boat a person usually takes. When the weather is bad and the ferry is late, people gather at his window, not at the notice board, because they trust what he tells them. He always knows the real schedule, even before the company posts it. Tourists sometimes shout at him about delays, but he answers slowly and politely, and they soon calm down. He could have retired years ago, but he says the harbour would feel empty without him. His job looks small from the outside: he just takes coins and gives little paper tickets. Yet without him, the mornings on the island would not run the same way. People do not always notice the ones who keep ordinary things working until those people are gone.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Present perfect for duration up to now
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu 'Kostas has sold ferry tickets... for almost forty years' dùng hiện tại hoàn thành với 'for' để diễn tả một việc bắt đầu trong quá khứ và vẫn tiếp tục đến hiện tại.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Kostas has sold ferry tickets... for almost forty years' uses present perfect with 'for' to express something that started in the past and continues now.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: could have + past participle for an unrealised possibility
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu 'He could have retired years ago' nói về một điều đã có thể xảy ra trong quá khứ nhưng đã không xảy ra. Dùng 'could have + quá khứ phân từ'.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'He could have retired years ago' talks about something that was possible in the past but did not happen, using 'could have + past participle'.

sampleTranslationVi: Kostas đã bán vé phà từ cùng một quầy nhỏ trong gần bốn mươi năm. Cái quầy nằm cạnh bến cảng trên một hòn đảo Hy Lạp, và lớp sơn quanh nó đã chuyển sang màu xanh nhạt vì muối biển và nắng. Mỗi sáng ông mở cửa cuốn đúng sáu giờ rưỡi, trước khi chuyến phà đầu tiên đến. Ông là một người điềm tĩnh và kiên nhẫn, và ông không bao giờ có vẻ vội vàng, ngay cả khi hàng người xếp dài. Ông biết gần như mọi người trên đảo theo tên, và ông thường nhớ một người hay đi chuyến phà nào. Khi thời tiết xấu và phà đến muộn, người ta tụ tập ở quầy của ông, chứ không phải ở bảng thông báo, vì họ tin những gì ông nói với họ. Ông luôn biết lịch trình thật, ngay cả trước khi công ty đăng nó lên. Khách du lịch đôi khi quát ông về việc chậm trễ, nhưng ông trả lời chậm rãi và lịch sự, và họ sớm bình tĩnh lại. Ông đã có thể nghỉ hưu nhiều năm trước, nhưng ông nói bến cảng sẽ có cảm giác trống vắng nếu thiếu ông. Công việc của ông trông nhỏ bé từ bên ngoài: ông chỉ nhận tiền xu và đưa những tấm vé giấy nhỏ. Vậy mà nếu không có ông, những buổi sáng trên đảo sẽ không vận hành theo cách như vậy. Người ta không phải lúc nào cũng để ý đến những người giữ cho những điều bình thường vận hành cho đến khi những người đó không còn nữa.

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the best-written sentence about how long he has worked.
options: Kostas has sold ferry tickets since almost forty years.
Kostas sells ferry tickets for almost forty years.
Kostas has sold ferry tickets for almost forty years.
Kostas has sell ferry tickets for almost forty years.
mcQuestions.explanation: Present perfect with 'for + period' shows duration up to now. 'Since' needs a start point; present simple loses the span; 'has sell' is wrong.
mcQuestions.hint: Which goes with a period of years: 'for' or 'since'?

mcQuestions.prompt: Pick the sentence describing his character naturally.
options: He is a calmly and patiently man.
He is a calm and patient men.
He is calm and patient a man.
He is a calm and patient man.
mcQuestions.explanation: Adjectives 'calm' and 'patient' modify the singular noun 'man'. Adverbs are wrong before a noun; 'men' is plural; word order in the last is broken.
mcQuestions.hint: Before a noun, do you use adjectives or adverbs?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence about his routine is best written?
options: Every morning he opens the shutter at half past six.
Every morning he open the shutter at half past six.
Every morning he is opening the shutter at half past six.
Every morning he opens the shutter on half past six.
mcQuestions.explanation: Present simple 'opens' fits a daily routine, with 'at' for clock time. 'Open' lacks -s; continuous is wrong for a habit; 'on' is the wrong preposition for a time.
mcQuestions.hint: Which preposition is used with a clock time?

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the sentence that uses 'never seems to' correctly.
options: He never seems hurry, even when the queue is long.
He never seems to hurry, even when the queue is long.
He never seems to hurrying, even when the queue is long.
He seems never to hurry, even when the queue is longer.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Seems to + base verb' (hurry) is correct. 'Seems hurry' drops 'to'; 'to hurrying' is wrong; 'longer' has no comparison partner.
mcQuestions.hint: After 'seems to', which verb form follows?

mcQuestions.prompt: Pick the sentence that says people trust his information, with 'because'.
options: People gather at his window because of they trust what he tells them.
People gather at his window because they trust what he tell them.
People gather at his window because they trust what he tells them.
People gather at his window, because trust what he tells them.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Because + subject + verb' with 'he tells' is correct. 'Because of' needs a noun; 'he tell' lacks -s; the last drops the subject.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'could have' for an unrealised past possibility?
options: He could have retire years ago.
He could retired years ago.
He could have retiring years ago.
He could have retired years ago.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Could have + past participle' (retired) shows a past possibility not taken. 'Have retire' and 'retiring' are wrong; 'could retired' omits 'have'.
mcQuestions.hint: After 'could have', which verb form is needed?

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the most natural sentence about how he handles angry tourists.
options: He answers slowly and politely, and they soon calm down.
He answers slow and polite, and they soon calm down.
He answers slowly and politely, and they soon calm.
He answer slowly and politely, and they soon calm down.
mcQuestions.explanation: Adverbs 'slowly' and 'politely' modify 'answers', and 'calm down' is the phrasal verb. Adjectives are wrong here; 'calm' alone is incomplete; 'answer' lacks -s.
mcQuestions.hint: Do you describe how he answers with adjectives or adverbs?

mcQuestions.prompt: Pick the sentence about the empty harbour using a conditional naturally.
options: He says the harbour will feel empty without him.
He says the harbour would feel empty without him.
He says the harbour would feels empty without him.
He says the harbour would felt empty without him.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Would feel' fits the hypothetical idea. 'Will feel' is too definite; 'would feels' double-marks; 'would felt' is wrong.
mcQuestions.hint: After 'would', which form of 'feel' is correct?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence describes his simple tasks naturally?
options: He just take coins and gives little paper tickets.
He just takes coins and give little paper tickets.
He just takes coins and gives little paper tickets.
He just takes coins and giving little paper tickets.
mcQuestions.explanation: Both present-simple verbs agree: 'takes' and 'gives'. The others drop the -s on one verb or use 'giving' without an auxiliary.
mcQuestions.hint: Both verbs share the subject 'he' — do they both need -s?

options: People do not always notice the ones who keeps ordinary things working.
People do not always notices the ones who keep ordinary things working.
People do not always notice the ones who keep ordinary things work.
People do not always notice the ones who keep ordinary things working.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'The ones who keep' agrees in the plural, and 'working' completes 'keep ... working'. 'Keeps' disagrees; 'notices' disagrees; 'work' breaks the 'keep ... -ing' pattern.
mcQuestions.hint: Does the relative clause 'who ___' agree with the plural 'ones'?

criticalThinkingQuestion: Why do small communities often depend on one quiet person more than they realise? What is lost when that person finally stops?
